---
title: "Statistical Models"
subtitle: What are they and why do we need them?
author: "Professor Andy Field"
institute: "University of Sussex"
format: 
  revealjs:
    auto-play-media: true
    template-partials:
      - title-slide.html
    title-slide-attributes:
      data-background-image: ../shared_media/images/slide_background_title.jpg
      data-background-size: 100%
      data-background-position: center
      data-background-opacity: "1"
execute: 
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

```{r}
# general
library(easystats)
library(tidyverse)
# specific
library(htmltools)


source("../helpers/discovr_helpers.R")
```


##  Learning outcomes 

::: incremental

- Articulate why we fit statistical models
- Understand the function and form of the linear model
    - The mathematical model
    - Visualizing the model
    - Familiar models as variants of the linear model
- List the common properties of all statistical models
- Understand what the model parameters (*b*s) represent
- Understand why we use sampling

:::



##

```{r, child = "spine_map_models.qmd"}

```


## 

::: {.callout-caution icon = false}
## {{< fa brain >}} Hypothesis

- Does attending concerts harm hearing?
:::



:::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](media/andy_download.png)
:::

::: {.column width="10%"}
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
![](media/zoe_download.png)
:::
:::



## Why do we fit models?


![](media/ds5_fig_02.02_fitting_models.png){fig-align="center" height=600}



## {background-video="media/bridge_01.mp4" background-size="cover"}

## {background-video="media/bridge_water.mp4" background-size="cover"}

## {background-video="media/bridge_wind.mp4" background-size="cover"}

## {background-video="media/bridge_hippo.mp4" background-size="cover"}


## {background-iframe="https://milton-the-cat.rocks/lm_sampling/" background-interactive=true}

::: notes
Let's return to our question. How do we answer it?
We build a model to unveil the truth, but there will be uncertainty.
But What model do we fit?
:::

# Why do (some) students hate statistics?

## {background-video="media/dw_42_clip.mp4" background-size="cover"}

##

::: {.r-stack}
::: {.fragment .fade-out}
::: txt_mulberry
::: txt_300
$$
Y_i = \hat{b}_0 + \hat{b}_1\text{X}_{i} + \text{e}_i
$$
:::
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-out}
::: txt_mulberry
::: txt_300
$$
r = \frac{\sum\limits_{i = 1}^n(x_i-\bar{X})(y_i-\bar{Y})}{(N-1)s_xs_y}
$$
:::
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-out}
::: txt_mulberry
::: txt_300
$$
t = \frac{\bar{X}_1-\bar{X}_2}{\sqrt{\frac{s_1^2}{n_1} + \frac{s_2^2}{n_2}}}
$$
:::
:::
:::

::: {.fragment .fade-in-then-out}
::: txt_mulberry
::: txt_300
$$
\chi^2 = \sum\frac{(\text{observed}_{ij}-\text{model}_{ij})^2}{\text{model}_{ij}}
$$
:::
:::
:::
:::


# Can we make statistics (a bit) less like this?

## {background-video="media/andy_dungeon_scream.mp4" background-size="cover"}

## {background-image="media/andy_zombie.jpg" background-size="cover"}

:::{.txt_xl}
:::{.txt_white}
A Zombie Quiz
:::
:::

```{r, child = "zombie_quiz.qmd"}

```



## Spoiler! We are always fitting this model

### The General Linear Model (GLM) 

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{outcome}_i &= (\text{model}_i) + \text{error}_i \\
\text{outcome}_i &= b_0 + b_1\text{predictor}_{i} + \text{error}_i \\
\text{outcome}_i &= b_0 + b_1\text{predictor 1}_{i} + b_2\text{predictor 2}_{i} + \dots + \text{error}_i
\end{aligned}
$$


:::{.callout-tip icon=false}
## {{< fa list-ul >}} All models have a S.P.I.N.E

::: incremental
- [**P**]{.alt}arameters. The $b_0$, $b_1$ ... $b_n$ are the model parameters.
- [**E**]{.alt}stimation: model parameters are estimated from the data ($\hat{b}_0$, $\hat{b}_1$ ... $\hat{b}_n$)
- [**S**]{.alt}tandard errors: there is uncertainty around these estimates because they vary from sample to sample. The standard error quantifies by how much they vary across samples.
- [**I**]{.alt}interval: the standard error can be used to quantify uncertainty using a confidence interval
- [**N**]{.alt}HST: we can test hypotheses using significance test of these parameter estimates (which also involved the standard error)
:::

:::


## Fitting models 

:::{.callout-note icon=false}
## {{< fa question >}} Questions to ask yourself

- What variable is my outcome? What type is it?
- What variable(s) are my predictors? What types are they?
- What does the resulting form of the model?
- Is it a good representation of the phenomenon?
- Is this model a good fit of the data?
:::

\


### Fitting models is [E.V.I.L.]{.alt}

::: incremental
- [**L**]{.alt}ook and [**L**]{.alt}oad: get the data into `r rproj()`, process it, summarize it (look)
- [**V**]{.alt}isualise: plot relevant information to understand the data/model
- [**E**]{.alt}valuate: is the model any good? Are its assumptions met? Does it 'fit' the data?
- [**I**]{.alt}nterpret: use the model to answer your question
:::

##

![](../shared_media/images/dsr2_fig_04_39_workflow.png){fig-align="center" height=600}


## Let's return to our question

::: {.callout-caution icon = false}
## {{< fa brain >}} Hypothesis

- Does attending concerts harm hearing?
:::



:::: columns
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](media/andy_download.png)
:::

::: {.column width="10%"}
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
![](media/zoe_download.png)
:::
:::


## The General Linear Model (GLM)

::: fragment

### What variable is my outcome? What type is it?

- Ringing in ears (minutes)
- Continuous

:::
::: fragment

### What variable(s) are my predictors? What types are they?

- Volume (db)
- Continuous

:::

::: fragment

### What is my model?

::: center-h
::: txt_mulberry
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{outcome}_i &= (\text{model}_i) + \text{error}_i \\
\text{outcome}_i &= b_0 + b_1\text{predictor}_{i} + \text{error}_i \\
\text{ringing}_i &= b_0 + b_1\text{volume}_{i} + e_i
\end{aligned}
$$
:::
:::
:::

# Collect data

##

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

htmltools::HTML('<section data-background-iframe="https://milton-the-cat.rocks/lm_sampling/" data-background-interactive> </section>')
```

::: notes
Let's return to our question. How do we answer it?
We build a model to unveil the truth, but there will be uncertainty.
But What model do we fit?
:::

# Use the data to estimate the model parameters

## Parameters and parameter estimates 

### The unobservable truth (population)

::: center-h
::: txt_mulberry
::: txt_150
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ringing}_i &= b_0 + b_1\text{volume}_{i} + \varepsilon_i
\end{aligned}
$$
:::
:::
:::

::: fragment

### The observable truth (sample)

::: center-h
::: txt_mulberry
::: txt_150
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ringing}_i &= \hat{b}_0 + \hat{b}_1\text{volume}_{i} + e_i
\end{aligned}
$$
:::
:::
:::
:::

\ 

::: fragment

### What are the parameter estimates?

- $\hat{b}_1$: Estimate of the parameter for a predictor
	- Direction/strength of relationship/effect between the predictor and the outcome (when other variables are constant)
	- Difference in means when predictors are categorical
- $\hat{b}_0$: the intercept
  - Estimate of the value of the outcome when predictor(s) = 0

:::


## How are parameters estimated?

### Ordinary Least Squares (OLS)

![](media/ols_animate.gif){fig-align="center" height=500}


## Visualise: What does the model look like?

![](../shared_media/images/v_hex.png){.absolute top=0 left=800 height="80"}

```{r}
#| echo: false

set.seed(666)

lm_pop <- tibble::tibble(
  id = stringi::stri_rand_strings(100, 5),
  volume = round(rnorm(id, mean = 105, sd = 5)),
  ears = round(10.4 + 10*volume  + rnorm(volume, mean = 0, sd = 25)),
  musician = ifelse(volume > median(volume), 1, 0)
)
```


::: center-h
::: txt_mulberry
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ringing}_i &= \hat{b}_0 + \hat{b}_1\text{volume}_{i} + e_i
\end{aligned}
$$

:::
:::

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false

lm_plot <- ggplot2::ggplot(lm_pop, aes(y = ears, x = volume)) +
  geom_point(colour = blue, alpha = 0.6, size = 2) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", fill = mulberry, colour = mulberry) +
  labs(x = "Volume of concert (db)", y = "Ears ringing (mins)") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(85, 120), ylim = c(850, 1200)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(85, 120, 5)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(850, 1200, 50)) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 18) 
lm_plot
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
:::
::::

## Visualise

![](../shared_media/images/v_hex.png){.absolute top=0 left=800 height="80"}

::: center-h
::: txt_mulberry
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ringing}_i &= \hat{b}_0 + \hat{b}_1\text{volume}_{i} + e_i
\end{aligned}
$$

:::
:::

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false

lm_plot_b1<- lm_plot +
  annotate("text", x = 119, y = 1025, label = list(bquote(hat(italic(b))[italic(1)])), size=7, parse = T) +
  annotate("segment", x = 115, y = 925, xend = 115, yend = 1150, colour = green, arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.03, "npc"), ends = "both"),  size = 1)

lm_plot_b1
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
:::
::::



## Visualise

![](../shared_media/images/v_hex.png){.absolute top=0 left=800 height="80"}

::: center-h
::: txt_mulberry
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ringing}_i &= \hat{b}_0 + \hat{b}_1\text{volume}_{i} + e_i
\end{aligned}
$$

:::
:::

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false

lm_plot_b1
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

ears_lm <- lm(ears~volume, data = lm_pop)
ears_pred_tib <- tibble::tibble(
  volume = seq(0, 95, 5),
  ears = predict(ears_lm, newdata = data.frame(volume = volume))
)

lm_plot_b0 <- lm_plot +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0, 120), ylim = c(-50, 1200)) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, 120, 10)) +
  scale_y_continuous(breaks = seq(-100, 1200, 100))

lm_plot_b0
```
:::
::::

## Interpret: what does the model mean?

![](../shared_media/images/i_hex.png){.absolute top=0 left=800 height="80"}

::: center-h
::: txt_mulberry
$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{\text{ringing}}_i &= -37.12 + 10.45\text{volume}_{i}
\end{aligned}
$$

:::
:::

:::: columns
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false

lm_plot_b1
```

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
```{r}
#| echo: false
#| message: false
#| warning: false

lm_plot_b0 +
  geom_line(aes(x = volume, y = ears), data = ears_pred_tib, fill = mulberry, colour = mulberry, linetype = 'longdash') +
  annotate("text", x = 0, y = 200, label = list(bquote(hat(italic(b))[italic(0)])), size=7, parse = T) +
  annotate("segment", x = 0, y = 145, xend = 0, yend = -20, colour = green, arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.03, "npc")),  size = 1) +
  annotate("text", x = 119, y = 575, label = list(bquote(hat(italic(b))[italic(1)])), size=7, parse = T) +
  annotate("segment", x = 115, y = 0, xend = 115, yend = 1150, colour = green, arrow = arrow(length = unit(0.03, "npc"), ends = "both"),  size = 1)
```
:::
::::


- $\hat{b}_1 = 10.45$:
	- Direction/strength of relationship/effect between volume and ringing
	- As volume goes up by 1db, we can expect 10.45 more minutes of ringing
- $\hat{b}_0 = -37.12$:
  - For how long we can expect ears to ring when volume is 0db (silence)
  - In silence, ears ring for -37.12 minutes `r emo::ji("thinking")`


::: notes
Note that the intercept is a value that extrapolates well beyond the data.
We might want to take it with a pinch of salt.
The intercept is -37.12. That makes no sense - your ears can't ring for a minus amount of time. You'd expect the value to be zero in the population (0 db = 0 ringing). Illustrates that estimates are not accurate. Also, is it reasonable to assume a linear relationship here? Isn't it more likely that a range of low volumes ringing will be zero minutes, and at a certain threshold ringing kicks in. It is almost certainly not linear along the whole range of volumes.
:::

# Sampling variation


## {background-iframe="https://milton-the-cat.rocks/lm_sampling/" background-interactive=true}


## {background-iframe="https://milton-the-cat.rocks/ci_explorer/" background-interactive=true}


## Confidence intervals

::: {.callout-tip icon = false}
## `r cat_space()` What they are:

- Intervals that contain the 'true' population value of the parameter in 95% of samples.

:::

\

::: fragment
::: {.callout-warning icon = false}
## `r bug()` What they are not:

- There is NOT a 95% probability that a given interval contains the population value.
    - It is *p* = 0 or *p* = 1, but you can’t know which!
- They do NOT reflect confidence in the value of the population parameter.

:::
:::


## Summary

- We fit statistical models to answer interesting questions
- All models are variations on the linear model
- Fitting models is E.V.I.L.
- Parameters tell us about the effects of interest
- We estimate these parameters from our data
- There will be uncertainty around those estimates
 - Confidence intervals quantify that uncertainty
